content.text: Eliza Labs Shutdown Announced: Shaw (shawmakesmagic) revealed that Eliza Labs is shutting down due to deteriorating market conditions, a treasury trending toward zero, and an inability to reach revenue targets. Shaw stated that all development and operations are ceasing but he intends to continue working on Eliza as an open source community project and offered to help displaced team members find new opportunities. Odilitime expressed disappointment but indicated willingness to contribute in a personal capacity. Community members raised concerns about the disconnect between what ElizaOS is building and its token value, with some emphasizing that clearer tokenomics and stronger messaging are critical to project survival.

Bitget Delisting and Token Sentiment: Bitget announced the delisting of the ELIZAOS/USDT trading pair on April 17, 2026, as part of a periodic review affecting seven trading pairs. Odilitime clarified that the ElizaOS team had never communicated with Bitget and attributed the delisting to low trading volume rather than any misconduct. He acknowledged that the team had failed to communicate their vision effectively and that investor confidence had declined, stating the project may be a failed experiment if the market cannot be made to understand what they are building. Community members noted the official X account had not posted since March and called for a trusted community member to manage social media. The migration window from ai16z to elizaOS tokens was also confirmed as closed.

Token Price Pessimism and Broader Market Concerns: Community members expressed significant pessimism about ElizaOS token price performance throughout the week, noting a decline of more than 95 percent from highs. Some predicted further drops to price levels as low as 0.00023, with comparisons made to Ai16z and concerns about the 11 billion token supply. Others attributed the downturn to broader macroeconomic instability and geopolitical tensions. Some members urged holders to either sell, hedge with derivatives, or hold if they believed in the project fundamentals at its roughly 5.5 million USD market cap. Questions about token utility, buyback timing, and planned airdrops were raised but met with vague responses from moderators.

v2.x Agent Development and Developer Updates: Odilitime revealed he is working on new v2.x agents currently in the develop branch of the repository, undergoing testing before a release and announcement. The update is also integrated inside Milady and was confirmed to be open source. A developer integrating a custom dashboard with ElizaOS v2 shared findings on the Socket.IO messaging protocol after extensive debugging, noting that direct emits fail and the working pattern requires emitting a message event with a type field. A core developer pointed to the ElizaOS documentation site and noted they are currently deep in v3 development. Separately, a developer reported recurring errors with Ollama local model integration, with Odilitime recommending Gemma, gptoss, and Qwen as working options for local deployments.

AgentAudit Tool and On-Chain Agent Infrastructure: A tool called AgentAudit was introduced in the developer channel, providing immutable on-chain audit logs for AI agents and described as EU AI Act compliant. It is live on Arbitrum Sepolia and Mantle Sepolia, has a TypeScript SDK available, and an ElizaOS plugin was announced as coming soon. Separately, a developer shared details about Orbis implementing the x402 usage-based billing scheme for AI agents natively on Base, enabling agents to authorize a maximum spend and only pay for actual usage across LLM and compute APIs. Community discussions also continued around inter-agent coordination, the cold-start trust problem, and the use of on-chain credentials and Base L2 attestation sequences to build verifiable agent behavioral history.

Hatcher.host and elizabao_ai Projects: Community members highlighted Hatcher.host as a major project currently in free beta, described as a platform where users can deploy Eliza agents without needing to write code, manage servers, or acquire hardware. One user described deploying an ElizaOS agent on Hatcher.host as a smooth one-click process with Groq integration and live logs. The elizabao_ai project also attracted growing interest, with users asking what it is and what they can currently do with it. The project creator noted many people are asking about it and confirmed plans to integrate with ElizaCloud and create agents for prediction markets. A Hong Kong event featuring elizaok_bsc built on ElizaOS was also shared.

Repository Cleanup and Policy Standardization: On April 12, 2026, the ElizaOS project completed a major repository-wide cleanup, closing stale issues that were 8 or more months old, retiring V3 planning goals, and removing deprecated milestones across all repositories. The project also standardized its policy for third-party plugin development, directing contributors to independently host and publish their integrations under the elizaOS-plugins organization rather than submitting them to the core repository. A new registry entry for the hashlock plugin is also under review.

Core Framework Improvements: Development across the week introduced a new agent workspace to streamline startup processes and added multi-language support for Python and Rust to the core framework. Core runtime-composition APIs were refactored to support flexible character loading via file paths and configuration options. Task-completion assessment was integrated into the reflection evaluator pipeline to improve runtime accuracy, and duplicate action result formatting was removed from the RECENT_MESSAGES provider to reduce unnecessary token usage in LLM context management. Work in progress includes group addressee routing and anti-loop prompt guidance.

Plugin Updates: Anthropic, Discord, and Cross-Chain: Critical compatibility issues for Opus 4.x were resolved in the Anthropic plugin by migrating to ai-sdk v6 parameter naming conventions, renaming maxTokens to maxOutputTokens, and stabilizing model performance with adjusted temperature settings. A TypeScript build error in the Anthropic plugin was also resolved. Feature parity improvements for the Discord plugin are in progress, covering typing indicators, reactions, streaming, and slash commands. A pull request was submitted to ensure the Discord plugin correctly respects the MILADY_DISCORD_OWNER_USER_IDS_JSON setting. A new MangoSwap plugin was added to the registry to enable cross-chain swaps across more than 10 blockchain networks.

Open Issues Requiring Community Attention: Two discussions require urgent community input. The first concerns multi-agent coordination patterns, specifically around persistent peer relationships and on-chain credential verification to solve the cold-start trust problem. The second involves payment infrastructure design for AI agents, including identity verification and trust-based spend limits. A proposal was also raised to implement delegation chains for autonomous agents, introducing scoped authority, spend limits, and cascade revocation to improve trust models. The MCP server named eliza received an A security grade on Loaditout, successfully passing all 7 evaluated criteria.
